summary: Cathy O'Neil announces a project to develop a platform for open-source models, seeking examples of algorithmic models that can be made transparent and interactive. She's collaborating with a Python developer and has already filed a FOIA request for NYC's teacher value-added model. The post solicits suggestions for open-source models in areas like credit scoring, municipal ratings, and weather prediction. The core motivation is that models can only be truly understood, validated, and improved when people can interact with them directly.
key_points: O'Neil is building a platform to showcase and interact with open-source algorithmic models
She's actively seeking models through FOIA requests (NYC teacher evaluations) and existing open-source projects (municipal credit ratings)
The platform aims to build interactive tools that let people explore how models work
Models don't need to be perfect - seeing flaws and enabling improvement is part of the point
True understanding of a model requires the ability to fiddle with parameters and check robustness
money_quotes: You don't really know what a model does until you can interact with it.
You don't know if a model is robust unless you can fiddle with its parameters and check.
You don't know if a model is best possible unless you've let people try to improve it.
They don't have to be great, because the point is to see their flaws and possible to improve them.
They don't have to be creepy!
key_insight: Algorithmic transparency requires more than publishing code - it requires interactive tools that let people probe, test, and challenge how models actually behave.
